did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning model combining EfficientNetV2 (CNN) and Mamba State Space Model (SSM) for multi-class classification of dermoscopic images to diagnose skin diseases (Melanoma, Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer, and Inflammatory/Benign disorders).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel hybrid EfficientNetV2-Mamba architecture for multi-class classification of dermoscopic images. It includes a methodology section detailing dataset preparation (8,655 images), model design, training strategies, and evaluation metrics. The results section reports experimental comparisons against baseline models (ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0, ViT, standalone Mamba) and discusses performance metrics like accuracy, robustness, and generalization. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCIDS), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: EfficientNetV2-Mamba: A Novel Hybrid CNN- State Space Model Architecture for Multi-Class Classification of Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2026 9th International Conference on Computational Intelligence in Data Science (ICCIDS)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel hybrid deep learning architecture, EfficientNetV2-Mamba, for the multi-class classification of dermoscopic images to diagnose Melanoma, Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer, and Inflammatory/Benign disorders. Utilizing a curated dataset of 8,655 images from Roboflow, the model combines EfficientNetV2 for local feature extraction with a Mamba State Space Model to capture long-range spatial dependencies. The authors evaluated the proposed architecture against baseline models including ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0, Vision Transformer, and standalone Mamba. Experimental results demonstrated that the hybrid CNN-SSM approach consistently outperformed these state-of-the-art models in terms of accuracy, robustness, and generalization. The study concludes that integrating convolutional and sequence-based modeling offers a synergistic effect for reliable dermatological image analysis with potential for clinical decision support.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three major classes in the multi-class classification task. Count: 1,101 images in the dataset.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class diagnosis of skin diseases (Melanoma, Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer, Inflammatory/Benign disorders)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into three major classes (Melanoma, NMSC, Inflammatory/Benign) and six specific subtypes. The model outputs discrete diagnostic categories using a fully connected layer after global average pooling. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and a confusion matrix is reported.

datasets_used.name: Roboflow Dermoscopic Dataset
datasets_used.description: An open-source dataset of 8,655 dermoscopic and clinical skin images available on the Roboflow platform. The dataset underwent strict curation including resolution/visual quality checks, hash-based duplicate removal, and manual label confirmation. Images were preprocessed by downsampling to 256x256, normalizing to [0,1], and applying mild color calibration. Data augmentation included horizontal/vertical flipping, small rotations, zoom/scale distortions, and brightness/contrast changes.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientNetV2-Mamba hybrid model and baseline models (ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0, Vision Transformer, standalone Mamba) via 70% stratified sampling.
use.validation: Used for validation via 15% stratified sampling.
use.testing: Used for testing via 15% stratified sampling.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To split the dataset into training (70%), validation (15%), and testing (15%) subsets while maintaining class distribution.

statistical_methods_used.method: Repeated experiments with varying random seeds
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide statistical strength, reproducibility, and minimize the influence of random fluctuations in training.
To report performance measures across the five repeated runs for objective comparison between models.
To analyze classification performance, specifically diagonal accuracy and false positive/negative rates between malignant and benign classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, ANOVA). It reported performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) as mean and standard deviation over five repeated runs with different random seeds to ensure statistical strength and reproducibility, but did not perform inferential statistical tests to compare models.

Roboflow Dermoscopic Dataset.experiment: Multi-class classification of skin diseases (Melanoma, NMSC, Inflammatory/Benign)

EfficientNetV2-Mamba (Proposed).notes: Best performance in all metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC). Achieved high diagonal accuracy in confusion matrix with minimal false positives/negatives between malignant and benign classes. Average inference latency within real-time clinical screening range.

Roboflow Dermoscopic Dataset.notes: Results are reported as mean and standard deviation (μ-σ) over five repeated runs with different random seeds. The proposed model consistently outperformed baselines in accuracy, robustness, and generalization. Confusion matrix analysis showed high classification levels for all three categories. Misclassifications were primarily between Melanoma and Inflammatory/Benign lesions, often due to low contrast, non-uniform illumination, or ambiguous boundaries.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; EfficientNetV2-B3 used as backbone with optimized compound scaling.

architecture_details.backbone_modification: Final layers of the backbone removed to generate high-dimensional feature maps (H×W×C)
architecture_details.sequence_conversion: Feature maps reshaped into token sequences to linearize spatial features
architecture_details.state_space_module: Mamba State Space Model (SSM) with selective state-space formulation for linear-time global context modeling
architecture_details.hybrid_design: Combines EfficientNetV2 for fine-grained local feature extraction and Mamba SSM for long-range spatial dependencies

training_details.training_schedule: Early stopping based on validation subset

conclusions: The proposed EfficientNetV2-Mamba hybrid architecture, which combines EfficientNetV2 for local feature extraction and Mamba State Space Model for global sequence modeling, consistently outperformed baseline models (ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0, Vision Transformer, standalone Mamba, and other hybrid baselines) in terms of accuracy, robustness, and generalization.

The hybrid CNN-SSM approach demonstrated superior efficacy in multi-class classification of dermoscopic images for Melanoma, Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer, and Inflammatory/Benign disorders, achieving high diagonal accuracy in the confusion matrix with minimal false positives and negatives between malignant and benign classes.

Integrating convolutional feature extraction with state-space modeling offers a balanced and highly effective approach for skin disease classification, leveraging complementary strengths: EfficientNetV2 captures fine-grained local details (color, texture, asymmetry) while Mamba models long-range structural relationships with linear computational complexity.
The model exhibits strong computational efficiency and scalability compared to Vision Transformers, allowing for near real-time inference suitable for clinical screening applications.

Grad-CAM visualizations indicate that the EfficientNetV2-Mamba model focuses on the entire lesion area, including borders and surrounding tissue, supporting its interpretability and reliability for dermatological image analysis.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalization, as the dataset did not include rare or less common skin conditions, and computational efficiency was only tested on high-end hardware (NVIDIA RTX 4090), with no tests performed on low-end or edge devices.
Future work is identified as necessary to test the model on more diverse disease-specific cases and evaluate its efficiency on different hardware platforms for real-world deployment.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (Roboflow) with no external validation, raising concerns about generalizability to diverse clinical populations.
The dataset excludes rare or less common skin conditions, potentially limiting the model's applicability to broader dermatological scenarios.

Computational efficiency and inference latency were only evaluated on high-end hardware (NVIDIA RTX 4090); no tests were performed on low-end or edge devices, which are critical for real-world clinical deployment.
Formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests) was not performed; statistical strength was derived only from repeated experiments (5 runs) with varying random seeds.

Misclassifications primarily occurred between Melanoma and Inflammatory/Benign lesions, often due to low contrast, non-uniform illumination, or ambiguous boundaries, indicating sensitivity to image quality.
The paper does not report specific accuracy values for baseline models (ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0, ViT, etc.), only stating that the proposed model outperformed them.
Pretraining details for the EfficientNetV2 backbone are not explicitly reported, though EfficientNetV2-B3 was used.
The dataset contains a mix of dermoscopic and clinical images, which may introduce heterogeneity in imaging conditions not fully addressed by mild color calibration.
